# Saga: v0-bootstrap

Bring up COR24 Smalltalk v0 — a minimal integer-only Tinytalk-style
VM hosted entirely in COR24 BASIC v1, in the spirit of the
~1000-line BASIC-hosted Smalltalk evaluator that Alan Kay's group
built in 1972 (see `docs/research.txt`).

Constraint: no C, no Python, no Rust in this repository. The VM is
written in BASIC; the BASIC interpreter itself is a sibling project
(`../sw-cor24-basic`, written in Pascal) and is treated as a fixed
substrate.

## Goal

Three runnable demos that prove the object/message/dispatch model:
- D1: `3 + 4` evaluated as a real `SmallInteger>>+` send (prints 7).
- D2: a Counter class with `init`/`incr`/`value` (prints 2).
- D3: `5 < 10 ifTrue: [42] ifFalse: [0]` via True/False objects
  (prints 42).

## Steps

### 1. phase-1-bootstrap

Stand up everything needed to run D1 (`3 + 4`) end-to-end against
the sibling `../sw-cor24-basic` interpreter:

- `.gitignore`, `scripts/build.sh`, `scripts/run.sh` wrapping the
  sibling's `run-basic.sh`.
- Phase-2 smoke tests in `examples/smoke/` validating the four
  BASIC v1 substrate behaviours v0 depends on (see
  `docs/plan.md` § Phase 2).
- `src/vm.bas`: heap helpers, eval/frame stacks, dispatch loop,
  SEND, LOOKUP, primitives 1 and 5.
- `src/image_d1.bas`: install `SmallInteger>>+` (primitive 1).
- `examples/d1_add.bas`: driver that issues the `3 + 4` send.
- A sibling-substrate sanity check (build + run a single demo).

Also file the upstream feature requests against
`sw-cor24-basic` (see step `upstream-feature-requests` below) at
the end of this step so the BASIC agent can start work in parallel.

### 2. demo-d2-counter

Add a user-defined class with one instance variable and three
methods (`init`, `incr`, `value`). New file `src/image_d2.bas`,
driver `examples/d2_counter.bas`. Proves that messages dispatch
by *receiver class*.

### 3. demo-d3-boolean

Install True/False with `ifTrue:ifFalse:`. New file
`src/image_d3.bas`, driver `examples/d3_boolean.bas`. Proves that
control flow works without native `IF`.

### 4. mini-repl

Optional. A tiny non-general parser that accepts the three demo
grammars from `INPUT`. New file `src/repl.bas`. Skip the step
(or downscope it) if `phase-1-bootstrap` plus D2/D3 already fills
the 16 KB tokenised program area.

### 5. upstream-feature-requests

File one GitHub issue per BASIC v1 limitation in
`sw-cor24-basic`, ordered to enable parallel development. Each
issue links back to the v0 design (`docs/design.md` § 11) and
explains exactly which Smalltalk refactor it unblocks here.

Order (each unblocks a distinct, independently-mergeable refactor
in this repo, so the BASIC agent and this agent can work in
parallel):

1. **FR-2 DATA/READ/RESTORE** — unblocks rewriting `image_d*.bas`
   files (single largest source of repetitive POKE lines).
2. **FR-1 DIM integer arrays** — unblocks rewriting `vm.bas`

   PEEK/POKE-as-array patterns).

4. **FR-4 MOD operator** — unblocks cleanup of tag-bit

5. **FR-5 Bitwise AND/OR/XOR/SHL/SHR** — unblocks compact
   tag/format encoding for v1.
6. **FR-6 CONT after STOP** — unblocks an interactive
   single-stepper.

Each issue gets opened as soon as `phase-1-bootstrap` is done.

### 6. refactor-image-data (depends on FR-2 landing upstream)

When `sw-cor24-basic` ships `DATA`/`READ`/`RESTORE`, replace each
`image_d*.bas`'s POKE block with a `DATA` block + a single read

### 7. refactor-vm-arrays (depends on FR-1 landing upstream)

When `DIM` lands, replace PEEK/POKE-as-heap throughout `vm.bas`

### 8. refactor-dispatch-on (depends on FR-3 landing upstream)

### 9. refactor-mod-and-bits (depends on FR-4 and FR-5 landing)

### 10. debug-cont-stepper (depends on FR-6 landing upstream)

### 11. v0-release-notes

## Parallel-development contract

input.content:
# Step: phase-1-bootstrap

Stand up COR24 Smalltalk v0 to the point where demo D1 (`3 + 4`)
runs end-to-end through real Smalltalk message dispatch on the
sibling BASIC interpreter, AND file the upstream BASIC feature
requests so the BASIC repo agent can start work in parallel.

## Read first
- `docs/prd.md` (scope and constraints)
- `docs/architecture.md` (layer model, memory map)
- `docs/design.md` (object encoding, bytecode, dispatch — § 11
  has the BASIC v1 shortcomings that drive the FR list)
- `docs/plan.md` (phases 2-7)
- `../sw-cor24-basic/CLAUDE.md`, `../sw-cor24-basic/README.md`,
  `../sw-cor24-basic/src/basic.pas` (especially lines 110-160 and
  290-300 for PEEK/POKE word-store semantics)

## Constraints
- No C, Python, or Rust source in this repo.
- Only BASIC source under `src/`, `examples/`. Shell under
  `scripts/`.
- Don't modify anything in `../sw-cor24-basic`. File issues against
  it instead.

## Deliverables
1. `.gitignore` (build artifacts, OS noise).
2. `scripts/build.sh`: cat `src/vm.bas` + per-demo image header into
   `build/<demo>.bas`.
3. `scripts/run.sh`: build then invoke
   `../sw-cor24-basic/scripts/run-basic.sh build/<demo>.bas`.
4. `examples/smoke/peek_word_store.bas`,
   `examples/smoke/tag_bit.bas`,
   `examples/smoke/gosub_chain.bas`,
   `examples/smoke/prog_size.bas`. Each is a standalone BASIC
   program (does not need vm.bas) that confirms one substrate
   property the v0 design depends on. See `docs/plan.md` § Phase 2
   for what each one tests.
5. `src/vm.bas`: heap helpers (1000-1499), stack helpers
   (1500-1999), dispatch loop (2000-2999), opcode handlers
   (3000-3999), SEND/LOOKUP (4000-4499), primitives 1 (`+`) and
   5 (`print`) (4500-4999). Layout matches
   `docs/architecture.md` § 7. Liberal `REM` comments because the
   code is the teaching artefact.
6. `src/image_d1.bas`: install class table, install
   `SmallInteger>>+` (selector id 1, primitive 1).
7. `examples/d1_add.bas`: top-level driver that hand-assembles the
   `3 + 4` bytecode into the bytecode pool, calls the dispatch
   loop, prints `7`.
8. Run D1 via `scripts/run.sh d1_add` and confirm output is `7`.
9. File 6 GitHub issues against `sw-cor24-basic` for FR-1..FR-6 in
   the order listed in `docs/plan.md` § Upstream feature requests.
   Each issue:
   - Title: `[FR-N] <feature>`
   - Body links back to `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11
     and to the specific refactor step in this saga that the FR
     unblocks (refactor-image-data, refactor-vm-arrays,
     refactor-dispatch-on, refactor-mod-and-bits,
     debug-cont-stepper).
   - Includes a one-paragraph "why this matters for hosting
     Smalltalk in BASIC" so the request isn't generic.

## Definition of done
- D1 prints `7` via real `SmallInteger>>+` dispatch (not via
  `PRINT 3+4`).
- All four smoke tests run cleanly under sibling pv24t.
- Six issues open against `sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic` with the
  correct titles and labels.
- `git commit` of all generated source plus `.agentrail/` step
  artefacts.

input.content:
# Step: demo-d3-boolean

Add demo D3: `5 < 10 ifTrue: [42] ifFalse: [0]` evaluated through
True and False objects with their own `ifTrue:ifFalse:` methods,
proving that control flow in v0 does NOT use a native `IF`.

## Read first
- `docs/design.md` § 5 (bytecode), § 8 (selector id 9 =
  `ifTrue:ifFalse:`), § 11 (Boolean encoding).
- `docs/plan.md` § Phase 9.

## Approach
v0 cheats: `[42]` and `[0]` are method literals, not real
closures. Each `ifTrue:ifFalse:` body just `RETURN_TOP`s the
appropriate literal directly. The receiver's class (True or False)
selects which method runs.

## Deliverables
1. `src/image_d3.bas`: install methods on True (returns arg 0) and
   False (returns arg 1). Install `SmallInteger>><` (primitive 4)
   that pushes True/False ref.
2. `examples/d3_boolean.bas`: driver hand-assembled to evaluate
   `5 < 10 ifTrue: 42 ifFalse: 0`.
3. `scripts/run.sh d3_boolean` prints `42`.

## Definition of done
- D3 prints `42`.
- D1 and D2 still pass.

# Step: refactor-image-data (depends on FR-2 in sw-cor24-basic)

**BLOCKED until** `sw-cor24-basic` issue [FR-2] DATA/READ/RESTORE
ships and the sibling `scripts/build-basic.sh` produces a working
interpreter that supports it.

## Goal

Replace the explicit `POKE addr,val` blocks in `src/image_d1.bas`,
`src/image_d2.bas`, `src/image_d3.bas` with `DATA` blocks read by
a single small `READ` loop. No semantic change; the demos must
produce identical output.

## Verification
- D1, D2, D3 still produce 7, 2, 42.
- Each `image_d*.bas` is at least 30% shorter and noticeably more
  readable.

content.content:
#!/bin/bash
# run-basic.sh — Run a .bas file through the BASIC interpreter
# Usage: ./scripts/run-basic.sh examples/hello.bas
#
# Feeds the .bas file as UART input to the compiled interpreter.
# Uses pv24t (host-side p-code trace interpreter).
set -euo pipefail

BAS="${1:?Usage: $0 <file.bas>}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
PV24T="$REPO_DIR/../sw-cor24-pcode/target/release/pv24t"
P24="$REPO_DIR/build/basic.p24"

if [ ! -f "$P24" ]; then
  echo "build/basic.p24 not found — run ./scripts/build-basic.sh first" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Feed .bas content as UART input, strip all > prompts.
# Append EOT (\x04) so the final line terminates cleanly even if the
# file lacks a trailing newline after BYE — otherwise read_line blocks
# waiting for more UART input and the run appears to hang.
INPUT="$(cat "$BAS")"$'\n\x04'
# pv24t: -n 0 means unlimited. Override with BASIC_MAX_INSN if you want a cap
# (e.g. for tight CI loops). Interactive game demos can spin forever waiting
# on input, so the default has to be unlimited.
: "${BASIC_MAX_INSN:=0}"
"$PV24T" "$P24" -i "$INPUT" -n "$BASIC_MAX_INSN" 2>&1 | sed -E 's/^>+//' | grep -v '^$'

#!/bin/bash
# build.sh — Assemble a Smalltalk demo by concatenating image header,
# the VM, and the demo driver into a single .bas file under build/.
#
# Usage: ./scripts/build.sh <demo>
#   where <demo> is one of: d1_add, d2_counter, d3_boolean
#
# The output build/<demo>.bas is fed to the sibling BASIC interpreter
# by scripts/run.sh.
set -euo pipefail

DEMO="${1:?Usage: $0 <demo>  (d1_add|d2_counter|d3_boolean)}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"

VM="$REPO_DIR/src/vm.bas"
IMG="$REPO_DIR/src/image_${DEMO#d?_}.bas"
case "$DEMO" in
  d1_add)      IMG="$REPO_DIR/src/image_d1.bas" ;;
  d2_counter)  IMG="$REPO_DIR/src/image_d2.bas" ;;
  d3_boolean)  IMG="$REPO_DIR/src/image_d3.bas" ;;
  *) echo "unknown demo: $DEMO" >&2 ; exit 2 ;;
esac
DRV="$REPO_DIR/examples/${DEMO}.bas"
OUT="$REPO_DIR/build/${DEMO}.bas"

for f in "$VM" "$IMG" "$DRV"; do
  if [ ! -f "$f" ]; then
    echo "missing source: $f" >&2
    exit 1
  fi
done

mkdir -p "$REPO_DIR/build"

# Concatenation order matters. The image header POKEs class table
# and methods, the VM defines GOSUB-able subroutines (must come
# before the driver invokes them), the driver hand-assembles the
# top-level bytecode and starts the dispatch loop.
#
# Each fragment uses a non-overlapping line-number range; see
# docs/architecture.md section 7. We rely on that, not on cat
# order, for correctness.
cat "$IMG" "$VM" "$DRV" > "$OUT"
echo "wrote $OUT"

#!/bin/bash
# run.sh — Build a demo and run it through the sibling BASIC.
# Usage: ./scripts/run.sh <demo>
set -euo pipefail

DEMO="${1:?Usage: $0 <demo>  (d1_add|d2_counter|d3_boolean)}"
SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
REPO_DIR="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/.." && pwd)"
BASIC_DIR="$REPO_DIR/../sw-cor24-basic"

if [ ! -x "$BASIC_DIR/scripts/run-basic.sh" ]; then
  echo "sibling not found at $BASIC_DIR" >&2
  exit 1

input.content:
9000 REM === COR24 SMALLTALK V0 VM CORE =================================
9001 REM HEAP / STACKS / DISPATCH / SEND / PRIMITIVES.
9002 REM ALL HELPERS ARE GOSUB-ONLY -- LINEAR FALL-THROUGH STOPS AT THE
9003 REM DRIVER'S "END" BEFORE LINE 9000.  ARGUMENT PASSING IS BY
9004 REM SHARED VARIABLE; SEE COMMENT AT EACH HELPER FOR INPUT/OUTPUT.
9005 REM
9006 REM ADDRESS LAYOUT WITHIN THE BASIC PEEK/POKE SCRATCH RAM (0..1023):
9007 REM   0..15    NIL/TRUE/FALSE SINGLETONS
9008 REM   16..511  HEAP ARENA (BUMP-ALLOCATED VIA H)
9009 REM   512..639 BYTECODE POOL (1 BYTE PER WORD, 128 BYTES MAX)
9010 REM   640..703 METHOD DICTIONARY (16 ENTRIES * 4 WORDS)
9011 REM   704..735 SYMBOL TABLE (UNUSED IN V0)
9012 REM   736..751 CLASS TABLE (16 SLOTS)
9013 REM   752..767 RESERVED
9014 REM   768..895 EVAL STACK (128 WORDS)
9015 REM   896..991 FRAME STACK (16 FRAMES * 6 WORDS)
9016 REM   992..1023 VM SCRATCH
9017 REM
9018 REM REGISTER USE: P=PC, M=METHOD ADDR, L=METHOD LEN, R=RECEIVER,
9019 REM C=CLASS, S=ESTACK PTR, F=FSTACK PTR, O=OPCODE, G=SELECTOR,
9020 REM N=ARGC, H=HEAP ALLOC, E=ERROR, A/B/D/Q/T/U/V/W=SCRATCH.
9021 REM I/J ARE LOOP-LOCAL IN HELPERS; THE DRIVER USES X/Y/Z.
9022 REM
9100 REM --- ISINT: IN V, OUT T (1 IF SMALLINT, 0 IF POINTER) -------------
9110 T=V-(V/2)*2
9120 RETURN
9200 REM --- TOINT: IN V (TAGGED SMALLINT), OUT T = DECODED INT ------------
9210 T=(V-1)/2
9220 RETURN
9300 REM --- MKINT: IN V (RAW INT), OUT T = TAGGED REF --------------------
9310 T=V*2+1
9320 RETURN
9400 REM --- PADDR: IN V (TAGGED HEAP REF), OUT T = SCRATCH ADDRESS -------
9410 T=V/2
9420 RETURN
9500 REM --- CLASSOF: IN V, OUT T = CLASS ID -----------------------------
9510 GOSUB 9100
9520 IF T=1 THEN T=2
9530 IF T=2 THEN RETURN
9540 GOSUB 9400
9550 T=PEEK(T)
9560 RETURN
9700 REM --- ALLOC: IN C=CLASS, N=SIZE, OUT T = TAGGED REF ---------------
9710 IF H+3+N>512 THEN E=4
9720 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9730 POKE H,C
9740 POKE H+1,N
9750 POKE H+2,0
9760 T=H*2
9770 H=H+3+N
9780 RETURN
9900 REM --- EPUSH: IN V, EFFECT: STACK[S++] = V -------------------------
9910 IF S>=128 THEN E=4
9920 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9930 POKE 768+S,V
9940 S=S+1
9950 RETURN
9960 REM --- EPOP: OUT T = STACK[--S] ------------------------------------
9970 IF S<=0 THEN E=4
9980 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9985 S=S-1
9990 T=PEEK(768+S)
9995 RETURN
10100 REM --- INSTALL_SINGLETONS: REQUIRED ONCE AT BOOT -------------------
10110 REM PLACES NIL (CLASS 5), TRUE (CLASS 3), FALSE (CLASS 4) AT
10120 REM HEAP ADDRESSES 0, 4, 8.  H IS BUMPED PAST THEM TO 16.
10130 POKE 0,5
10140 POKE 1,0
10150 POKE 2,0
10160 POKE 4,3
10170 POKE 5,0
10180 POKE 6,0
10190 POKE 8,4
10200 POKE 9,0
10210 POKE 10,0
10220 POKE 12,6
10230 POKE 13,0
10240 POKE 14,0
10250 H=16
10260 RETURN
10300 REM --- INSTALL_METHOD: IN C=CLASS, G=SEL, A=BCSTART, B=BCLEN -------
10310 REM WALK METHOD DICT (640..703, 16 ENTRIES * 4 WORDS) FOR FIRST
10320 REM EMPTY SLOT (CLASS = -1 OR 0) AND WRITE THE ENTRY.
10330 W=0
10340 IF W>=16 THEN E=4
10350 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
10360 IF PEEK(640+W*4)<=0 THEN GOTO 10380
10370 W=W+1
10375 GOTO 10340
10380 POKE 640+W*4,C
10390 POKE 640+W*4+1,G
10400 POKE 640+W*4+2,A
10410 POKE 640+W*4+3,B
10420 RETURN
10500 REM --- LOOKUP: IN C=CLASS, G=SEL, OUT Q=ENTRY INDEX OR -1 ----------
10510 W=0
10520 IF W>=16 THEN Q=-1
10530 IF W>=16 THEN RETURN
10540 IF PEEK(640+W*4)<>C THEN GOTO 10570
10550 IF PEEK(640+W*4+1)<>G THEN GOTO 10570
10560 Q=W
10565 RETURN
10570 W=W+1
10580 GOTO 10520
10700 REM --- INIT_METHDICT: MARK ALL ENTRIES EMPTY (CLASS = -1) ---------
10710 W=0
10720 IF W>=16 THEN RETURN
10730 POKE 640+W*4,-1
10740 W=W+1
10750 GOTO 10720
12000 REM === DISPATCH LOOP ===============================================
12010 REM EXPECTS M, L, P, R PRE-SET BY CALLER.  RUNS UNTIL HALT (OP=0).
12020 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
12030 IF P>=L THEN RETURN
12040 O=PEEK(M+P)
12050 P=P+1
12060 IF O=0 THEN RETURN
12070 IF O=1 THEN GOSUB 13100
12080 IF O=2 THEN GOSUB 13200

9000 REM === COR24 SMALLTALK V0 VM CORE =================================
9001 REM HEAP / STACKS / DISPATCH / SEND / PRIMITIVES.
9002 REM ALL HELPERS ARE GOSUB-ONLY -- LINEAR FALL-THROUGH STOPS AT THE
9003 REM DRIVER'S "END" BEFORE LINE 9000.  ARGUMENT PASSING IS BY
9004 REM SHARED VARIABLE; SEE COMMENT AT EACH HELPER FOR INPUT/OUTPUT.
9005 REM
9006 REM ADDRESS LAYOUT WITHIN THE BASIC PEEK/POKE SCRATCH RAM (0..1023):
9007 REM   0..15    NIL/TRUE/FALSE SINGLETONS
9008 REM   16..511  HEAP ARENA (BUMP-ALLOCATED VIA H)
9009 REM   512..639 BYTECODE POOL (1 BYTE PER WORD, 128 BYTES MAX)
9010 REM   640..703 METHOD DICTIONARY (16 ENTRIES * 4 WORDS)
9011 REM   704..735 SYMBOL TABLE (UNUSED IN V0)
9012 REM   736..751 CLASS TABLE (16 SLOTS)
9013 REM   752..767 RESERVED
9014 REM   768..895 EVAL STACK (128 WORDS)
9015 REM   896..991 FRAME STACK (16 FRAMES * 6 WORDS)
9016 REM   992..1023 VM SCRATCH
9017 REM
9018 REM REGISTER USE: P=PC, M=METHOD ADDR, L=METHOD LEN, R=RECEIVER,
9019 REM C=CLASS, S=ESTACK PTR, F=FSTACK PTR, O=OPCODE, G=SELECTOR,
9020 REM N=ARGC, H=HEAP ALLOC, E=ERROR, A/B/D/Q/T/U/V/W=SCRATCH.
9021 REM I/J ARE LOOP-LOCAL IN HELPERS; THE DRIVER USES X/Y/Z.
9022 REM
9100 REM --- ISINT: IN V, OUT T (1 IF SMALLINT, 0 IF POINTER) -------------
9110 T=V-(V/2)*2
9120 RETURN
9200 REM --- TOINT: IN V (TAGGED SMALLINT), OUT T = DECODED INT ------------
9210 T=(V-1)/2
9220 RETURN
9300 REM --- MKINT: IN V (RAW INT), OUT T = TAGGED REF --------------------
9310 T=V*2+1
9320 RETURN
9400 REM --- PADDR: IN V (TAGGED HEAP REF), OUT T = SCRATCH ADDRESS -------
9410 T=V/2
9420 RETURN
9500 REM --- CLASSOF: IN V, OUT T = CLASS ID -----------------------------
9510 GOSUB 9100
9520 IF T=1 THEN T=2
9530 IF T=2 THEN RETURN
9540 GOSUB 9400
9550 T=PEEK(T)
9560 RETURN
9700 REM --- ALLOC: IN C=CLASS, N=SIZE, OUT T = TAGGED REF ---------------
9710 IF H+3+N>512 THEN E=4
9720 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9730 POKE H,C
9740 POKE H+1,N
9750 POKE H+2,0
9760 T=H*2
9770 H=H+3+N
9780 RETURN
9900 REM --- EPUSH: IN V, EFFECT: STACK[S++] = V -------------------------
9910 IF S>=128 THEN E=4
9920 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9930 POKE 768+S,V
9940 S=S+1
9950 RETURN
9960 REM --- EPOP: OUT T = STACK[--S] ------------------------------------
9970 IF S<=0 THEN E=4
9980 IF E<>0 THEN RETURN
9985 S=S-1
9990 T=PEEK(768+S)
9995 RETURN

10120 REM HEAP ADDRESSES 0, 4, 8.  H IS BUMPED PAST THEM TO 16.

10140 POKE 1,0
10150 POKE 2,0
10160 POKE 4,3
10170 POKE 5,0
10180 POKE 6,0
10190 POKE 8,4
10200 POKE 9,0
10210 POKE 10,0
10220 POKE 12,6
10230 POKE 13,0
10240 POKE 14,0
10250 H=16
10260 RETURN
10300 REM --- INSTALL_METHOD: IN C=CLASS, G=SEL, A=BCSTART, B=BCLEN -------
10310 REM WALK METHOD DICT (640..703, 16 ENTRIES * 4 WORDS) FOR FIRST
10320 REM EMPTY SLOT (CLASS = -1 OR 0) AND WRITE THE ENTRY.
10330 W=0

10370 W=W+1

10390 POKE 640+W*4+1,G
10400 POKE 640+W*4+2,A
10410 POKE 640+W*4+3,B
10420 RETURN
10500 REM --- LOOKUP: IN C=CLASS, G=SEL, OUT Q=ENTRY INDEX OR -1 ----------
10510 W=0
10520 IF W>=16 THEN Q=-1
10530 IF W>=16 THEN RETURN
10540 IF PEEK(640+W*4)<>C THEN GOTO 10570
10550 IF PEEK(640+W*4+1)<>G THEN GOTO 10570
10560 Q=W

10570 W=W+1

input.content:
## Summary

Add classic BASIC `DATA` / `READ` / `RESTORE` to COR24 BASIC v1.
This is the **first** of six feature requests filed from the
`sw-cor24-smalltalk` project (see "parallel-development context"
below for the full list and ordering rationale).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
100 DATA 13, 1, 8, 12, 3, 12, 4, 7, 1, 1
110 RESTORE 100
120 FOR I=0 TO 9
130 READ V
140 POKE 512+I, V
150 NEXT
```

- `DATA <expr> [, <expr>]...` — a numbered line that contributes
  integers (signed 24-bit, same as everything else) to a global
  read pointer.
- `READ <var>` — read one integer from the data pool, advance
  the read pointer.
- `RESTORE` — reset read pointer to the first DATA line.
- `RESTORE <line>` — reset read pointer to a specific DATA line.
- Out-of-data raises error code 13 (`OUT OF DATA`, already
  reserved in `docs/design.md` § 7.2).
- DATA values follow integer-literal syntax: optional sign, decimal
  digits. No string DATA in v1.

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The sibling `sw-cor24-smalltalk` project hosts a tiny Smalltalk VM
inside BASIC. The Smalltalk *image* (class table + method
dictionary + bytecode) is data that has to be loaded into BASIC's
PEEK/POKE scratch RAM at boot. With no `DATA` statement, today
the image-loader is a long sequence of explicit `POKE addr,val`
lines:

```basic
512 POKE 512,13
513 POKE 513,1
514 POKE 514,8
... (and another 60+ lines for D2/D3 images)
```

`DATA`/`READ` collapses this to one DATA block plus a six-line
read loop, which is dramatically more readable and uses ~40% less
of the 16 KB tokenised program area.

This unblocks the saga step `004-refactor-image-data` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`. That step is queued *now* and will
be picked up as soon as this FR ships, while subsequent BASIC
work continues on FR-1, FR-3, etc., in parallel.

## Cross-references

- Smalltalk-side design catalog: `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md`
  § 11.3 (FR-2).
- Smalltalk-side saga step that depends on this:
  `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/004-refactor-image-data`.

## Parallel-development context

Six feature requests are filed together (FR-1 through FR-6) in the
order in which the Smalltalk repo can absorb them in independent,
parallel-mergeable refactors. The Smalltalk agent will pick up each
refactor as soon as the corresponding FR is merged; the BASIC agent
should work them in this order so the parallel pipeline stays full:

1. **FR-2 DATA/READ/RESTORE** (this issue) — image loader cleanup.
2. **FR-1 DIM integer arrays** — VM heap/stack/dict cleanup.
3. **FR-3 ON expr GOTO/GOSUB** — bytecode dispatch loop.
4. **FR-4 MOD operator** — tag-bit extraction cleanup.
5. **FR-5 Bitwise AND/OR/XOR/SHL/SHR** — compact tag/format encoding.
6. **FR-6 CONT after STOP** — interactive VM stepper.

None blocks any other; each unlocks a distinct commit on the
Smalltalk side.

## Out of scope for this FR

- String DATA (`DATA "HELLO"`). v1 has no string variables, so
  string DATA wouldn't have a destination.
- `READ` into multi-target lists (could be a follow-up).

input.content:
## Summary

Add `DIM` integer arrays to COR24 BASIC v1. Second of six FRs
filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see FR-2 / issue #2 for the
parallel-development context).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
100 DIM A(15)         REM declare A as 16-element integer array (0..15)
110 DIM B(31), C(7)   REM multiple per DIM
120 LET A(3) = 42
130 PRINT A(3)
```

- `DIM <name>(<size>)` declares an integer array of `size+1`
  elements indexed `0..size`. Element type is the same signed
  24-bit integer as scalars.
- Single-letter names A..Z (re-using the scalar namespace, classic
  BASIC convention: `A` and `A()` are *distinct*).
- One dimension only in v1. Multi-dim is a v2 follow-up.
- Subscript out of range: error code 8 (`BAD ADDRESS`) — reuse
  existing.
- Re-DIMming an array clears it to zero.

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The Smalltalk VM in `sw-cor24-smalltalk/src/vm.bas` simulates its
heap, eval stack, frame stack, method dictionary, and class table
as offsets into the 1024-word PEEK/POKE scratch RAM. Every read
and write is a pair of arithmetic ops plus a `PEEK`/`POKE`. Real
arrays would replace lines like:

```basic
9920 IF S>=128 THEN E=4
9930 POKE 768+S, V        REM eval-stack push
9940 S=S+1
```

with:

```basic
9930 LET ESTACK(S) = V
9940 LET S = S + 1
```

Across the VM that is roughly 80 fewer lines of arithmetic and a
much clearer reading experience for educational use (the whole
point of the project — we want a reader to *see* message-send
work, not decode address arithmetic).

This unblocks saga step `005-refactor-vm-arrays` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`, which can be picked up the moment
this FR ships, while other BASIC FRs proceed in parallel.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-1).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/005-refactor-vm-arrays`.
- Already noted in `sw-cor24-basic/docs/plan.md` § 7 as a Pascal
  Phase-1 prerequisite — the same `DIM` requirement that the BASIC
  interpreter itself needed from p24p will benefit user code once
  exposed at the BASIC dialect level.

## Out of scope for this FR

- Multi-dimensional arrays.
- Dynamic re-DIM with size change preserving contents.
- String arrays (no string variables in v1).

input.content:
## Summary

Add `ON <expr> GOTO <line>[,<line>...]` and
`ON <expr> GOSUB <line>[,<line>...]` to COR24 BASIC v1. Third of
six FRs filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see #2 for context).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
100 ON OP+1 GOSUB 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000
```

- Evaluate `<expr>`. The 1-based result selects which target to
  branch to.
- Index `<= 0` or `>` length-of-list: fall through (no branch).
  This matches Microsoft BASIC and Dartmouth BASIC behaviour.
- `ON ... GOTO`: replace the current line pointer.
- `ON ... GOSUB`: push return-line, then branch (mirrors `GOSUB`).
- Maximum number of targets bounded by the 80-char line buffer
  (~12 four-digit line numbers comfortably).

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The Smalltalk VM's bytecode dispatch loop in
`sw-cor24-smalltalk/src/vm.bas` is currently a 14-deep `IF`-chain:

```basic
12070 IF O=1 THEN GOSUB 13100
12080 IF O=2 THEN GOSUB 13200
12090 IF O=3 THEN GOSUB 13300
... (and 11 more lines)
```

That's O(n) per opcode — every `PUSH_INT` pays the cost of all 14
comparisons even though only the last matches. With `ON` the
whole dispatch collapses to one line:

```basic
12060 ON O+1 GOSUB 13000,13100,13200,13300,13400,13500,13600, \
                  13700,13800,13900,14000,14100,14200,14300
```

That is O(1) dispatch and roughly 40 fewer source lines for the
VM core.

The same pattern then applies to the primitive trampoline
(currently another 6-deep IF chain at line 14310). After this FR
the VM's hot path is two `ON ... GOSUB` lines.

This unblocks saga step `006-refactor-dispatch-on` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-3).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/examples/smoke/gosub_chain.bas` — reproduces
  the 14-deep dispatch pattern at small scale; useful for any

## Summary

Add `ON <expr> GOTO <line>[,<line>...]` and
`ON <expr> GOSUB <line>[,<line>...]` to COR24 BASIC v1. Third of
six FRs filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see #2 for context).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
100 ON OP+1 GOSUB 1000, 2000, 3000, 4000, 5000
```

- Evaluate `<expr>`. The 1-based result selects which target to
  branch to.
- Index `<= 0` or `>` length-of-list: fall through (no branch).
  This matches Microsoft BASIC and Dartmouth BASIC behaviour.
- `ON ... GOTO`: replace the current line pointer.
- `ON ... GOSUB`: push return-line, then branch (mirrors `GOSUB`).
- Maximum number of targets bounded by the 80-char line buffer
  (~12 four-digit line numbers comfortably).

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The Smalltalk VM's bytecode dispatch loop in
`sw-cor24-smalltalk/src/vm.bas` is currently a 14-deep `IF`-chain:

```basic
12070 IF O=1 THEN GOSUB 13100
12080 IF O=2 THEN GOSUB 13200
12090 IF O=3 THEN GOSUB 13300
... (and 11 more lines)
```

That's O(n) per opcode — every `PUSH_INT` pays the cost of all 14
comparisons even though only the last matches. With `ON` the
whole dispatch collapses to one line:

```basic
12060 ON O+1 GOSUB 13000,13100,13200,13300,13400,13500,13600, \
                  13700,13800,13900,14000,14100,14200,14300
```

That is O(1) dispatch and roughly 40 fewer source lines for the
VM core.

The same pattern then applies to the primitive trampoline
(currently another 6-deep IF chain at line 14310). After this FR
the VM's hot path is two `ON ... GOSUB` lines.

This unblocks saga step `006-refactor-dispatch-on` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-3).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/examples/smoke/gosub_chain.bas` — reproduces
  the 14-deep dispatch pattern at small scale; useful for any
  before/after timing the BASIC project wants to record.
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/006-refactor-dispatch-on`.

## Out of scope for this FR

- Computed `GOSUB` from a variable list rather than a literal one.
- `ON ERROR GOTO` (separate, larger feature).

input.content:
## Summary

Add the `MOD` operator to COR24 BASIC v1's expression parser.
Fourth of six FRs filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see #2 for
context).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
100 LET T = V MOD 2
110 IF (I MOD 15) = 0 THEN PRINT "FIZZBUZZ"
```

- Same precedence as `*` and `/`.
- `A MOD B` returns `A - (A/B)*B`. Sign of result follows the
  truncating-toward-zero `/` already in v1.
- `A MOD 0` raises error code 5 (`DIVISION BY ZERO`).
- Token byte: next free in the operator block (0xAA suggested).

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The Smalltalk VM's tag-bit extractor (the `ISINT` predicate) is
the single hottest helper — it runs on every classed value
lookup, every push, every primitive entry. Today, with no `MOD`
or bitwise operators, the BASIC source is:

```basic
9100 REM --- ISINT: IN V, OUT T (1 IF SMALLINT, 0 IF POINTER) ---
9110 T = V - (V/2)*2
```

With `MOD` it becomes:

```basic
9110 T = V MOD 2
```

The runtime difference is small but the *readability* difference
is large, and this helper appears in the project's teaching
material (`sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 2.1) as a worked
example of "how to extract a tag bit when the host language has
no bit operations." Once `MOD` lands, that example tightens.

There's also a correctness benefit: the existing
`V - (V/2)*2` form gives `-1` for `V = -3` (verified in
`examples/smoke/tag_bit.bas`), which means tag-bit extraction
quietly malfunctions on negative refs. The `MOD` form (assuming
the standard truncating semantics above) has the same caveat,
but is clearer about *why*.

This unblocks half of saga step `007-refactor-mod-and-bits` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`. The other half (FR-5 bitwise ops)
is independent and can land separately.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-4).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/examples/smoke/tag_bit.bas` — demonstrates
  the workaround currently in use.
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/007-refactor-mod-and-bits`.

## Out of scope for this FR

- Floored vs truncated modulo flavour (the request follows v1's
  existing `/` semantics).
- Bitwise operators — those are FR-5, separate issue.

input.content:
## Summary

Add bitwise operators / functions to COR24 BASIC v1: bitwise
`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, plus shift operators `SHL` and `SHR`. Fifth
of six FRs filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see #2 for context).

## Proposed dialect

The existing `AND`/`OR` keywords are *logical* (truthy/falsy).
Two viable approaches; the BASIC project picks:

**Option A — overload existing keywords on integer args.**

If both operands are `0` or `1`, behaviour is unchanged.
Otherwise both keywords work bitwise. Simple but conflates the
two meanings.

**Option B — new keywords `BAND`, `BOR`, `BXOR`, `SHL`, `SHR`.**

```basic
100 LET T = V BAND 1            REM tag bit
110 LET T = (V SHR 1)           REM untag heap pointer
120 LET T = (X SHL 8) BOR Y     REM pack two bytes
```

Recommend Option B — it keeps logical and bitwise distinct, which
matters for the v1 dialect's teaching value.

Token bytes: next free in the operator block (0xAB..0xAF range
has room).

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The v0 Smalltalk VM uses arithmetic to extract and set tag bits
because BASIC v1 has no bit ops. Today:

```basic
9110 T = V - (V/2)*2     REM ISINT: extract bit 0
9210 T = (V-1)/2         REM TOINT: shift right + adjust
9310 T = V*2 + 1         REM MKINT: shift left + tag
9410 T = V/2             REM PADDR: shift right
```

With bit ops:

```basic
9110 T = V BAND 1
9210 T = V SHR 1
9310 T = (V SHL 1) BOR 1
9410 T = V SHR 1
```

These four helpers run on essentially every operation in the VM,
so the impact is wide if not deep. More importantly, the
*future* Smalltalk v0.1 wants to pack the bytecode pool more
densely (4 bytes per word instead of 1 byte per word) and that
requires `SHL`/`SHR` to extract the right byte from a packed
word. That refactor is currently impossible without these ops.

This unblocks the second half of saga step
`007-refactor-mod-and-bits` in `sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`,
plus enables a future `008-pack-bytecode-pool` step.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-5).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 6 (current 1-byte-per-word
  bytecode pool that this FR enables compressing).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/007-refactor-mod-and-bits`.

## Out of scope for this FR

- Bitwise NOT (`BNOT`) — easy to add in a follow-up if needed.
- Rotate operators.

input.content:
## Summary

Add `CONT` to resume execution after `STOP`. Sixth and last of
the FR set filed from `sw-cor24-smalltalk` (see #2 for context).

## Proposed dialect

```basic
> 10 LET A = 1
> 20 STOP
> 30 LET A = A + 1
> 40 PRINT A
> RUN
STOPPED IN 20
> PRINT A
1
> CONT
2
```

- `CONT` resumes from the line *after* the most recent `STOP`.
- If no `STOP` has executed since the last `RUN`/`NEW`, `CONT`
  reports `?CAN'T CONTINUE` (new error code in the existing
  table — code 16 is free).
- Variables, GOSUB stack, FOR stack must all be preserved across
  `STOP`. (Today they already are, until the next `RUN` resets
  them. The change is just adding the resume entry-point.)
- Editing a stored line between `STOP` and `CONT` invalidates
  resume: subsequent `CONT` reports `?CAN'T CONTINUE`.

This is documented in `sw-cor24-basic/docs/prd.md` § 6 as a v2
candidate; this FR moves it to v1.x.

## Why this matters for hosting Smalltalk in BASIC

The v0 Smalltalk VM is meant to be a *teaching* artefact:
educators and learners walk through a Smalltalk message send and
see exactly what happens at each bytecode. Today the VM dispatch
loop runs to completion (or error) without exposing intermediate
state.

With `CONT`, the VM can be wired with a "step mode" flag whose
dispatch loop emits `STOP` after each opcode:

```basic
12060 IF O=0 THEN RETURN
12061 IF STEPMODE=0 THEN GOTO 12070
12062 STOP
```

Now the BASIC user can interactively `PRINT P,M,R,S,V` between
opcodes, then `CONT` to run one more. That turns the BASIC REPL
into a single-step Smalltalk debugger almost for free — and
debugging is the original Smalltalk pitch ("you can fix any bug
without leaving the system").

This unblocks saga step `008-debug-cont-stepper` in
`sw-embed/sw-cor24-smalltalk`. It is the lowest-priority FR in
the set: D1, D2, D3 all run today without `CONT`. Land it last.

## Cross-references

- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/docs/design.md` § 11.3 (FR-6).
- `sw-cor24-basic/docs/prd.md` § 6 (already documented as v2
  candidate; this FR proposes promoting it).
- `sw-cor24-smalltalk/.agentrail/steps/008-debug-cont-stepper`.

## Out of scope for this FR

- Breakpoints (`BREAK <line>`). A natural follow-up but not
  needed for the smalltalk stepper, which uses a flag the VM
  itself reads.
- `STEP` keyword for line-by-line execution (the FR-6 caller can
  approximate it with `STOP` + `CONT`).

# COR24 Smalltalk v0 — Status

_Updated: 2026-04-24_

## What exists in this repo today

- `CLAUDE.md` — agentrail instructions (renamed from
  `AGENTS.example.md`, per the agentrail bootstrap rules).
- `COPYRIGHT`, `LICENSE` — MIT.
- `docs/research.txt` — the conversation that defined the project.
- `docs/process.md` — copied from `sw-cor24-basic`. Note: it is
  Rust-flavoured. v0 does **not** use Rust; treat the
  process.md as the *workflow shape* (TDD, pre-commit gates,
  documented learnings) and ignore the cargo-specific commands
  until the doc is rewritten for the BASIC toolchain.
- `docs/tools.md`, `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` — also copied
  from the BASIC repo; same caveat.
- `docs/prd.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/design.md`,
  `docs/plan.md` — written this session.
- `docs/status.md` — this file.

There is no `src/` and no `.bas` source yet. No build script. No
agentrail saga. No tests.

## What does not exist yet

- Anything that runs. Phase 1 is documentation only.
- `src/vm.bas`, `src/image_d1.bas`, etc.
- `scripts/build.sh` to concatenate the source files.
- An agentrail saga (the `.agentrail/` directory). When the user
  is ready to start work, the next step is `agentrail init` per
  the rules in `CLAUDE.md`.
- A `README.md`. By convention we will write that after the first
  demo runs.
input.new_string:
# COR24 Smalltalk v0 — Status

_Updated: 2026-04-24 (saga step 001-phase-1-bootstrap)_

## What runs today

- **Demo D1: `3 + 4` -> `7`** via real `SmallInteger>>+` dispatch
  through `CLASSOF -> LOOKUP -> ACTIVATE -> PRIM 1`. Run with
  `./scripts/run.sh d1_add`. This is the project's first claim
  of being "Smalltalk".
- All four substrate smoke tests in `examples/smoke/` pass under
  the sibling `pv24t`:
  - `peek_word_store.bas`: PEEK/POKE below 1024 stores full 24-bit
    *signed* words including negatives. (Confirmed; see § "Reality
    check" below for the parser-overflow gotcha discovered while
    writing it.)
  - `tag_bit.bas`: `V - (V/2)*2` extracts the low bit correctly
    for non-negative refs.
  - `gosub_chain.bas`: 14-deep `IF GOSUB` dispatch over 100
    iterations runs in ~30 ms via `pv24t`. Adequate for the demos;
    motivates FR-3 only if we go to bigger images.
  - `prog_size.bas`: 200 `POKE` lines plus loops fit easily in the
    16 KB tokenised program area.

## What exists in this repo

- `CLAUDE.md`, `COPYRIGHT`, `LICENSE`.
- `docs/{prd,architecture,design,plan,status}.md`,
  `docs/research.txt` (research transcript inherited),
  `docs/process.md`, `docs/tools.md`,
  `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` (placeholders inherited from
  the BASIC repo; cargo-flavoured, will be rewritten when the
  Smalltalk toolchain stabilises).
- `.agentrail/` saga `v0-bootstrap` with 9 steps:
  001-phase-1-bootstrap (in progress, this commit closes it),
  002 demo-d2-counter, 003 demo-d3-boolean, 004
  refactor-image-data (gated on FR-2), 005 refactor-vm-arrays
  (gated on FR-1), 006 refactor-dispatch-on (gated on FR-3),
  007 refactor-mod-and-bits (gated on FR-4 + FR-5), 008
  debug-cont-stepper (gated on FR-6), 009 v0-release-notes.

    motivates FR-3 only if we go to bigger images.

    16 KB tokenised program area.

# COR24 Smalltalk v0 — Status

_Updated: 2026-04-24_

## What exists in this repo today

- `CLAUDE.md` — agentrail instructions (renamed from
  `AGENTS.example.md`, per the agentrail bootstrap rules).
- `COPYRIGHT`, `LICENSE` — MIT.
- `docs/research.txt` — the conversation that defined the project.
- `docs/process.md` — copied from `sw-cor24-basic`. Note: it is
  Rust-flavoured. v0 does **not** use Rust; treat the
  process.md as the *workflow shape* (TDD, pre-commit gates,
  documented learnings) and ignore the cargo-specific commands
  until the doc is rewritten for the BASIC toolchain.
- `docs/tools.md`, `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` — also copied
  from the BASIC repo; same caveat.
- `docs/prd.md`, `docs/architecture.md`, `docs/design.md`,
  `docs/plan.md` — written this session.
- `docs/status.md` — this file.

There is no `src/` and no `.bas` source yet. No build script. No
agentrail saga. No tests.

## What does not exist yet

- Anything that runs. Phase 1 is documentation only.
- `src/vm.bas`, `src/image_d1.bas`, etc.
- `scripts/build.sh` to concatenate the source files.
- An agentrail saga (the `.agentrail/` directory). When the user
  is ready to start work, the next step is `agentrail init` per
  the rules in `CLAUDE.md`.
- A `README.md`. By convention we will write that after the first
  demo runs.

## Reality check on the substrate

- `sw-cor24-basic` builds and runs — see its `README.md`. It
  ships Hello World, factorial, fizzbuzz, Star Trek, etc., so
  the integer-only BASIC v1 feature set is real and verified.
- v0 of this project relies on five things that the substrate
  already provides:
  1. Integer expressions and `LET`.
  2. `IF`, `GOTO`, `GOSUB`/`RETURN`, `FOR`/`NEXT`.
  3. `PEEK`/`POKE` against scratch addresses 0..1023, which
     actually store full 24-bit *words* (not bytes) for that
     range. See `../sw-cor24-basic/src/basic.pas` line 120 / 298
     and our `docs/design.md` § 11.2 #13.
  4. `PRINT` / `INPUT`.
  5. 16 KB tokenised program area (`PS=16384`).
- Known substrate bug: `ABS` is broken (sw-cor24-basic#1). v0
  does not use `ABS`.

## Open BASIC v1 feature requests we might file

Captured in `docs/plan.md` § "Upstream feature requests" and
`docs/design.md` § 11.3. None is a v0 blocker, but FR-1 (`DIM`)
and FR-3 (`ON GOTO`) would meaningfully shrink the v0 source.

## Immediate next step

1. Run the four Phase-2 smoke tests against the BASIC v1
   interpreter (`docs/plan.md` § Phase 2). Especially #2.1, the
   "PEEK/POKE below 1024 stores 24-bit words" test, since the
   entire heap design depends on it.
2. If all four pass, start Phase 3 (`src/vm.bas` lines 1000-1499).
3. If any fail, file the corresponding upstream issue in
   `sw-cor24-basic` and pause until fixed.

## Out of scope (also see `prd.md` § 6)

- A general Smalltalk parser, real Block closures, GC,
  metaclasses, image save/load, the visual tile editor.
- Any C, Python, or Rust source in this repo.

## Decision log

- 2026-04-24: project created.
- 2026-04-24: hosting language for v0 is BASIC (the sibling
  `sw-cor24-basic` v1 dialect). The Smalltalk VM will be a
  ~1000-line BASIC program, mirroring the historical 1972
  BASIC-hosted Smalltalk evaluator described in
  `docs/research.txt`.
- 2026-04-24: heap and dictionaries live in BASIC's PEEK/POKE
  scratch RAM (1024 24-bit words). The 26 BASIC variables A-Z
  serve as VM registers (mapping in `architecture.md` § 3).
- 2026-04-24: bytecode set frozen at 14 opcodes for v0
  (`design.md` § 5).
- 2026-04-24: BASIC v1 shortcomings catalogued; upstream FRs
  drafted but not yet filed (`design.md` § 11, `plan.md`
  § "Upstream feature requests").

input.new_string:
## Reality check on the substrate

All five substrate behaviours v0 depends on are now empirically
verified, not just inferred from `basic.pas`:

1. Integer expressions and `LET` — every demo uses these.
2. `IF`, `GOTO`, `GOSUB`/`RETURN`, `FOR`/`NEXT` — exercised by
   smoke tests and the VM dispatch loop.
3. `PEEK`/`POKE` 0..1023 store full 24-bit signed words
   (-8388608..+8388607), confirmed by `peek_word_store.bas`.
   *Caveat discovered in this step*: BASIC v1's integer-literal
   parser silently overflows on values > 8388607 (e.g. the
   literal `16777215` is parsed via repeated `n*10` accumulation
   and wraps), so any image needing the full positive range must
   compute the value in BASIC rather than write it as a literal.
   The smoke test now uses 8388607, 1048576, 65536, 255, -1.
4. `PRINT` of a SmallInt outputs no leading space and a trailing
   newline — perfect for primitive 5 (`print`).
5. The 16 KB tokenised program area easily holds image + VM +
   driver: D1's combined source is 346 lines, well under the cap.

Known substrate bug: `ABS` is broken (sw-cor24-basic#1). v0
does not use `ABS`.

# COR24 Smalltalk v0 — Status

_Updated: 2026-04-24 (saga step 001-phase-1-bootstrap)_

## What runs today

- **Demo D1: `3 + 4` -> `7`** via real `SmallInteger>>+` dispatch
  through `CLASSOF -> LOOKUP -> ACTIVATE -> PRIM 1`. Run with
  `./scripts/run.sh d1_add`. This is the project's first claim
  of being "Smalltalk".
- All four substrate smoke tests in `examples/smoke/` pass under
  the sibling `pv24t`:
  - `peek_word_store.bas`: PEEK/POKE below 1024 stores full 24-bit
    *signed* words including negatives. (Confirmed; see § "Reality
    check" below for the parser-overflow gotcha discovered while
    writing it.)
  - `tag_bit.bas`: `V - (V/2)*2` extracts the low bit correctly
    for non-negative refs.
  - `gosub_chain.bas`: 14-deep `IF GOSUB` dispatch over 100
    iterations runs in ~30 ms via `pv24t`. Adequate for the demos;
    motivates FR-3 only if we go to bigger images.
  - `prog_size.bas`: 200 `POKE` lines plus loops fit easily in the
    16 KB tokenised program area.

## What exists in this repo

- `CLAUDE.md`, `COPYRIGHT`, `LICENSE`.
- `docs/{prd,architecture,design,plan,status}.md`,
  `docs/research.txt` (research transcript inherited),
  `docs/process.md`, `docs/tools.md`,
  `docs/ai_agent_instructions.md` (placeholders inherited from
  the BASIC repo; cargo-flavoured, will be rewritten when the
  Smalltalk toolchain stabilises).
- `.agentrail/` saga `v0-bootstrap` with 9 steps:
  001-phase-1-bootstrap (in progress, this commit closes it),
  002 demo-d2-counter, 003 demo-d3-boolean, 004
  refactor-image-data (gated on FR-2), 005 refactor-vm-arrays
  (gated on FR-1), 006 refactor-dispatch-on (gated on FR-3),
  007 refactor-mod-and-bits (gated on FR-4 + FR-5), 008
  debug-cont-stepper (gated on FR-6), 009 v0-release-notes.
- `scripts/{build,run,run-bare}.sh`.
- `src/vm.bas` — heap helpers, eval stack, dispatch loop, SEND,
  primitives 1-6 (with primitives 2/3/4/6 untested until D2/D3).
- `src/image_d1.bas` — installs `SmallInteger>>+`.
- `examples/d1_add.bas` — top-level driver.
- `examples/smoke/*.bas` — substrate smoke tests.

## Upstream feature requests filed

In priority order (each unblocks a distinct, parallel-mergeable
saga step here):

| FR | sw-cor24-basic issue | Unblocks step |
|---|---|---|
| FR-2 DATA/READ/RESTORE | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#2 | 004-refactor-image-data |
| FR-1 DIM arrays | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#3 | 005-refactor-vm-arrays |
| FR-3 ON GOTO/GOSUB | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#4 | 006-refactor-dispatch-on |
| FR-4 MOD operator | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#5 | 007-refactor-mod-and-bits |
| FR-5 Bitwise ops | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#6 | 007-refactor-mod-and-bits |
| FR-6 CONT after STOP | sw-embed/sw-cor24-basic#7 | 008-debug-cont-stepper |

Parallel-development contract: as soon as any FR merges and ships
in `../sw-cor24-basic`, this repo's corresponding refactor step
unblocks and can run independently. The BASIC agent works the FRs
in the order above; this agent picks up whichever refactor
matches whatever just shipped.

## What does not exist yet

- Demos D2 (Counter) and D3 (Boolean) — saga steps 002 and 003.
  The VM scaffolding for both is in `vm.bas` but stubs only
  (PUSH_FIELD/STORE_FIELD/PUSH_TEMP/JUMP*/RETURN_TOP all set
  E=1).
- Mini source REPL.
- A `README.md` (by convention, written after D2/D3 ship).

## Reality check on the substrate

All five substrate behaviours v0 depends on are now empirically
verified, not just inferred from `basic.pas`:

1. Integer expressions and `LET` — every demo uses these.
2. `IF`, `GOTO`, `GOSUB`/`RETURN`, `FOR`/`NEXT` — exercised by
   smoke tests and the VM dispatch loop.
3. `PEEK`/`POKE` 0..1023 store full 24-bit signed words
   (-8388608..+8388607), confirmed by `peek_word_store.bas`.
   *Caveat discovered in this step*: BASIC v1's integer-literal
   parser silently overflows on values > 8388607 (e.g. the
   literal `16777215` is parsed via repeated `n*10` accumulation
   and wraps), so any image needing the full positive range must
   compute the value in BASIC rather than write it as a literal.
   The smoke test now uses 8388607, 1048576, 65536, 255, -1.
4. `PRINT` of a SmallInt outputs no leading space and a trailing
   newline — perfect for primitive 5 (`print`).
5. The 16 KB tokenised program area easily holds image + VM +
   driver: D1's combined source is 346 lines, well under the cap.

Known substrate bug: `ABS` is broken (sw-cor24-basic#1). v0
does not use `ABS`.

## Open BASIC v1 feature requests we might file

Captured in `docs/plan.md` § "Upstream feature requests" and
`docs/design.md` § 11.3. None is a v0 blocker, but FR-1 (`DIM`)
and FR-3 (`ON GOTO`) would meaningfully shrink the v0 source.

## Immediate next step

1. Run the four Phase-2 smoke tests against the BASIC v1
   interpreter (`docs/plan.md` § Phase 2). Especially #2.1, the
   "PEEK/POKE below 1024 stores 24-bit words" test, since the
   entire heap design depends on it.
2. If all four pass, start Phase 3 (`src/vm.bas` lines 1000-1499).
3. If any fail, file the corresponding upstream issue in
   `sw-cor24-basic` and pause until fixed.

## Out of scope (also see `prd.md` § 6)

- A general Smalltalk parser, real Block closures, GC,
  metaclasses, image save/load, the visual tile editor.
- Any C, Python, or Rust source in this repo.

## Decision log

- 2026-04-24: project created.
- 2026-04-24: hosting language for v0 is BASIC (the sibling
  `sw-cor24-basic` v1 dialect). The Smalltalk VM will be a
  ~1000-line BASIC program, mirroring the historical 1972
  BASIC-hosted Smalltalk evaluator described in
  `docs/research.txt`.
- 2026-04-24: heap and dictionaries live in BASIC's PEEK/POKE
  scratch RAM (1024 24-bit words). The 26 BASIC variables A-Z
  serve as VM registers (mapping in `architecture.md` § 3).
- 2026-04-24: bytecode set frozen at 14 opcodes for v0
  (`design.md` § 5).
- 2026-04-24: BASIC v1 shortcomings catalogued; upstream FRs
  drafted but not yet filed (`design.md` § 11, `plan.md`
  § "Upstream feature requests").

git -c commit.gpgsign=false commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Bootstrap v0: D1 (3+4) runs end-to-end via real message dispatch

First saga step (001-phase-1-bootstrap) of the v0-bootstrap saga.
Stands up COR24 Smalltalk v0 to the point where 3 + 4 evaluates
through the full Tinytalk path: PUSH_INT/SEND, CLASSOF on the
tagged SmallInteger receiver, LOOKUP in the method dictionary,
ACTIVATE inlining the primitive (since the method body starts
with opcode 13), PRIM 1 popping/summing/pushing, and finally
PRIM 5 PRINT printing 7.

Also files six upstream feature requests against sw-cor24-basic
(issues #2-#7) in priority order for parallel development:
FR-2 DATA/READ (#2), FR-1 DIM (#3), FR-3 ON GOTO/GOSUB (#4),
FR-4 MOD (#5), FR-5 bitwise ops (#6), FR-6 CONT (#7). Each
unblocks a distinct, parallel-mergeable refactor step in this
saga (steps 004-008).

Deliverables:
- docs/{prd,architecture,design,plan,status}.md
- agentrail saga with 9 steps (001..009)
- scripts/{build,run,run-bare}.sh (text concatenation build,
  delegating execution to ../sw-cor24-basic/scripts/run-basic.sh)
- examples/smoke/{peek_word_store,tag_bit,gosub_chain,prog_size}.bas
  empirically verifying the four BASIC v1 substrate behaviours
  v0 depends on (especially: PEEK/POKE 0..1023 stores full 24-bit
  signed words, not bytes, including negatives)
- src/vm.bas: heap helpers (ISINT/TOINT/MKINT/PADDR/CLASSOF),
  bump-pointer ALLOC, eval stack push/pop, dispatch loop, SEND,
  LOOKUP, ACTIVATE (primitive-method short-circuit), primitives
  1-6 (only 1 and 5 exercised by D1)
- src/image_d1.bas: installs SmallInteger>>+ (selector id 1) at
  bytecode pool offset 512
- examples/d1_add.bas: hand-assembles the top-level bytecode and
  invokes the dispatch loop

D2 (Counter) and D3 (Boolean ifTrue:ifFalse:) are queued as steps
002 and 003 of the saga; their VM scaffolding is reserved in
vm.bas (PUSH_FIELD/STORE_FIELD/PUSH_TEMP/JUMP*/RETURN_TOP all
return E=1 for now).

Constraint honoured: no C, Python, or Rust source in this
repository. The VM is BASIC; the BASIC interpreter itself is
Pascal in the sibling project.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -5
